title: Cat Swollen Fluid Pocket on Ear Flap: Triage Guide for Aural Hematomas
meta_desc: Noticed a fluid-filled swelling on your cat's ear? It may be an aural hematoma. Learn about symptoms, urgency levels, and why photos help your vet.
content_html:
What is the Swollen Pocket on My Cat's Ear?

A soft, fluid-filled swelling on a cat's ear flap is most commonly an aural hematoma. This condition occurs when small blood vessels within the ear cartilage rupture, usually due to trauma or self-injury. The blood pools between the skin and the ear cartilage, creating a pillow-like or balloon-like appearance that may feel warm or firm to the touch.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for an aural hematoma is Medium. While it is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is a painful and uncomfortable condition that requires a veterinary visit within 24 to 48 hours. If left untreated, the fluid may eventually be reabsorbed, but the ear is likely to shrivel and develop a permanent deformity known as cauliflower ear, which can obstruct the ear canal and lead to chronic infections.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo of your cat's ear is incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. A photo allows the vet to see the initial size and location of the swelling, which is useful for monitoring whether it is growing rapidly. It also helps the vet distinguish between a hematoma and other potential issues like an abscess or an insect sting before you even step into the clinic.

faqs.question: What causes aural hematomas in cats?
faqs.answer: They are typically caused by trauma from vigorous head shaking or scratching. This is often triggered by an underlying issue such as ear mites, an ear infection, or skin allergies.

faqs.question: Can I drain the fluid pocket at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to drain an ear hematoma at home. Doing so introduces a high risk of infection and the pocket will likely refill immediately. Professional veterinary care is required to address the source of the bleeding.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to treat it?
faqs.answer: Treatment usually involves draining the fluid, often through a small incision or needle aspiration. In many cases, surgery is required to place sutures that prevent the pocket from refilling, alongside treating the underlying cause of the head shaking.
